abstract.t: This document defines an information modeling language designed to improve the rigor of Internet technical specifications. Existing data standards like ABNF, XSD, and JSON Schema define formal syntax for text and structured data values. But while RFC 3444 and RFC 8477 establish goals for information modeling, adoption remains limited. JSON Abstract Data Notation (JADN) addresses this by defining a formal syntax for information using a uniform type structure that balances simplicity with extensibility and expressive power. The defining feature of any IM is equivalence across data formats, but unlike traditional schema languages, a JADN schema is itself an information value. This ensures equivalence across schema representations, allowing authors to select or design a representation suitable for the application and target audience.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: begins: "There has been ongoing confusion about the differences between Information Models and Data Models for defining managed objects in network management." The confusion can be partially attributed to the absence of a definition of information itself, so this specification begins with two:

dl.dd: structured, processed data that has been given context, meaning, and semantics within a specific domain. It turns raw facts into usable knowledge by organizing data objects, their attributes, and their relationships, typically at a high level of abstraction, independent of physical storage protocols.
the reduction of uncertainty, measured as the statistical rarity of a message rather than its semantic meaning. It quantifies how much a data point reduces doubt, measured in bits.

section.t: Given a data value, the information it conveys can be viewed as the questions it answers. An information model (IM) defines equivalence between messages in different formats, and a concise message such as an RFC 791 IP packet contains the identical information (both semantics: "What is the source address?" and its entropy: "32 bits") as an equivalent verbose message in JSON or XML format.
An implementable IM specification must also define Information Value, Data Value, and the relationships between them:

dl.dd: the value of a variable (a typed unit of state) in a processing environment. In this specification information values are displayed with strings in single quotes to distinguish them from similar-looking JSON data values.

a fixed sequence of bytes usable outside a processing environment. Also known as a Document, Message, File, Stream, Literal, or Lexical Value. Because messages are immutable they can be compared for equality and hashed or signed for integrity.

definition of the content of an information or data value of that type. An IM type has three properties: an information value space, a literal space for each data format, and a small set of functions and relations including ordering of the value space and mappings from each literal space to the value space. This definition extends the XML Schema Datatype to information modeling by including the literal spaces and mappings for multiple data formats. Both information and data values are instances of Type.

the implementation of a variable in a processing environment. An object has a type, a current value, and the behavior defined for variables of that type. Objects are instances of Class. Note that this is not specific to object-oriented programming languages: K&R C has an "int" type and its implementation of int variables (the int class) has behavior such as addition and comparison in addition to the value space defined by the int type. Also note that JavaScript defines Boolean, String, Number, Array, Map and Set objects in chapters 20-24. JSON confusingly uses "object" to refer only to the values of Map objects, not any others. Objects in this specification have values of all types as in JavaScript, not just Maps as in JSON.

a named equivalence mapping between information values and literal data values. More than one data format can be defined for a single media type, for example Record information values can be represented as either Map or Array literals in the same media type, depending on data format.
Two data values are equivalent if and only if they are the same type and are mapped to the same information value.

section.name: Objectives
section.t: The objectives of JADN are:

li.t: The IM must be abstract, unambiguously defining information values at a high level that separates information sharing requirements from implementation details.

ul.li: Implementation details include programming language and data format.
The IM is a high-level definition of structured information that can be used as formal pseudocode in documentation and standards even if it is not used to process data.

Readability for the widest possible audience is JADN's highest priority. Humans are the primary audience for IMs; DMs are machine-oriented and may be less suited to understanding and explaining structured information.

li.t: An IM schema must itself be an information value to support representation in multiple formats.

ul.li: Presenting a schema in a format familiar to the audience aids understanding. Schema-specific formats (e.g., property tables, entity relationship diagrams) may be more readable to some audiences than general data formats.

An IM schema can also be encoded in a general data format and can either accompany or be referenced by the data it defines. CBOR has a requirement that "Data must be able to be decoded without a schema description", but CBOR data that includes a CBOR schema is both self-describing and more concise than repeating descriptions in every instance. For the Coordinate example, a JADN schema in CBOR format defines "latitude" and "longitude" elements once so that CBOR data can encode coordinate values as Major type 4, a tuple of length 2, not repeatedly including descriptions in every coordinate. A Coordinate schema in CDDL format is less suitable for this purpose because it is not CBOR data.

li.t: The IM must unambiguously define equivalence between information values and data values in most data formats used in Internet standards.

ul.li: The schema must be composed of data types, not classes or objects, because only types define the mapping between external and internal values. Segregating mapping methods into encoding rules allows the schema to be abstract.
The encoding rules that define a data format must be schema-independent to promote data format re-use, and unambiguous to ensure equivalence between formats.

li.t: The IM must be comprehensive. Information values must be able to represent message structures used in most Internet processing environments.

ul.li: Associative arrays (maps, dictionaries, hashes) are universally used in programming but are not defined as types in UML, ASN.1, or XSD. JADN defines a Map type.
Other common processing types such as sets, ordered maps and unordered lists are used less frequently but must be available in the IM for use when needed.

li.t: The IM language should be both conceptually simple and easily extensible to promote integration with existing communication mechanisms.

ul.li: In addition to its use as pseudocode, an IM can serve as a single source of truth when generating multiple DMs.

An unlimited variety of formally standardized, coalition sponsored, or company developed DM languages are used in the Internet. Translation among DM languages is not necessarily lossless, but a structurally uniform IM language that covers the significant overlap across them simplifies translation to, and reverse engineering from, DMs in those languages.

ol.li: The IM language should be usable for modeling data directly, to address cases where existing DM languages are non-existent (e.g., IP packets) or insufficient (e.g., JSON Schema's inability to express ordered map semantics).

section.name: Examples
section.t: Example representations of a trivial GPS Coordinate type illustrate that a JADN schema is an information value, that that value can be represented in multiple data formats, and that the information value is identical regardless of format. The formats shown are:

ul.li: JSON data: the normative storage format for JADN schemas
XML data: an XML format composed of JADN elements, called XML Abstract Schema Definition (XASD) language.
Text: JADN Information Definition Language (IDL), an example non-normative domain-specific language (DSL).
Property Tables: a widely-used data standards publication format, shown as Markdown source. Tables can also be represented in HTML, XML and office document data formats.

figure.name: JADN XASD Format
figure.artwork: <?xml version="1.0" ?> <Schema version="2.0"> <Types> <Type name="Coordinate" type="Record">GPS Coordinate <Field fid="1" fname="latitude" type="Number" minInclusive="-90.0" maxInclusive="90.0">Latitude</Field> <Field fid="2" fname="longitude" type="Number" minExclusive="-180.0" maxInclusive="180.0">Longitude</Field> </Type> </Types> </Schema>

figure.name: JADN IDL Format
figure.artwork: Coordinate = Record // GPS Coordinate 1 latitude Number=[-90.0, 90.0] // Latitude 2 longitude Number=(-180.0, 180.0] // Longitude

figure.name: JADN Property Table, Markdown Format
figure.artwork:
**Type: Coordinate (Record):** GPS Coordinate
| ID | Name | Type | \# | Description | |----|---------------|------------------------|----|-------------| | 1 | **latitude** | Number=[-90.0, 90.0] | 1 | Latitude | | 2 | **longitude** | Number=(-180.0, 180.0] | 1 | Longitude |

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Types
section.t: A JADN schema is composed exclusively of type definitions, not a mixture of types and other mechanisms sometimes called classes in other data modeling approaches. Every type definition has the identical five-element structure:

ol.li: TypeName: the name of the type being defined.
CoreType: The JADN built-in type of the type being defined.
TypeOptions: a set of zero or more TypeOption values applicable to the CoreType (conditional).
Fields: an array of zero or more Item or Field definitions as applicable to the CoreType (conditional).

section.t: This uniform type structure along with a namespaced package header makes a JADN IM capable yet easy to process. Currently-defined TypeOptions and FieldOptions provide significant expressive power, and unlimited space for new options enables easy extensibility.

JADN IM types are structured as a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Definitions can be arranged in hierarchical tiers for readability, and common types can be reused in multiple types. This differs from tree graphs where types must always be acyclic and denormalized, and from mesh graphs that make no distinction between contained and referenced content. Cross-references (links / foreign keys) in a DAG support cyclic relationships where needed, but are the exception rather than the rule. Data must live somewhere, and only a model that distinguishes values from references specifies where in a document or message to find it.

The types in this specification are well-known abstract concepts such as "integer", "sequence" and "map". They are not exhaustively defined here because many other publications provide excellent definitions of these concepts. However, this specification will attempt to describe these types well enough that they can be readily recognized and distinguished from other abstractions with which they may be confused.
JADN defines information values and message syntax based on twelve Core types in three categories:

ul.li: Primitive: Five types where each instance is an atomic value. The Fields array is empty in primitive type definitions.

Compound: Five types where each instance is a collection of values. The Fields array is empty for unstructured compound types (ArrayOf, MapOf) and has the same five-element Field structure for all structured compound types (Array, Map, Record).

Union: Two types where each instance is selected from a set of possible values. Each Field is a three-element Item structure for the Enumerated type, and the five-element Field structure for the Choice type.

section.t: The Item structure for Enumerated types is:
The Field structure for Compound and Choice types is:

section.name: Options
section.t: lists all TypeOptions and FieldOptions defined in this specification. Their meaning and usage is described under specific types in the following sections. Each option has a numeric ID, a name, and a value type. In JSON format options are serialized as a list of strings using "tagged string" encoding for brevity. In option tagged strings the Unicode codepoint of the first character is the option ID and the remaining characters are the string representation of its value. Tagged strings also highlight that options are inherently enumerated, not arbitrarily-named attributes. Tagged string encoding can also be used for general data by defining a translation table similar to .

In the GPS Coordinate example, latitude has options represented in JSON format as ["y-90.0", "z90.0"]. From the first character "y" (U+0079 = ID 121) has name "minInclusive", the remaining characters are the value string "-90.0", and the type "BType" indicates that the value type is the base type of the field (Number). The information value of the latitude options is {'minInclusive': -90.0, 'maxInclusive': 90.0}:
section.sourcecode: +----+-----------+ TaggedString TypeOption = "y-90.0" (String) | ID | Value | ID = 121 = U+0079 = lower case "y" +----+-----------+ Info: Name = "minInclusive", Value = -90.0 (Number)
section.t: The more verbose XASD representation of latitude options: <type="Number" minInclusive="-90.0" maxInclusive="90.0"> is equivalent to the to the JSON representation because both are mapped to the same information value.

figure.name: TypeOptions and FieldOptions
figure.artwork:
ID TypeOption Type ID Description ___ __________ _______ ___ __________________________________________ 61 id Boolean =: Fields indexed by FieldId, not FieldName 42 valueType String *: Value for ArrayOf and MapOf is referenced type 43 keyType String +: Key for MapOf is referenced type 35 enum String #: Enumeration derived from referenced type 62 path String >: Paths derived from referenced type 37 pattern String %: String matches regular expression 117 default BType u: Default value 118 const BType v: Constant value 119 minExclusive BType w: minimum value, excluding bound 120 maxExclusive BType x: maximum value, excluding bound 121 minInclusive BType y: minimum value 122 maxInclusive BType z: maximum value 123 minLength Integer {: minimum string length or collection count 125 maxLength Integer }: maximum string length or collection count 113 unique Boolean q: ArrayOf instance is an OrderedSet 115 set Boolean s: ArrayOf instance is a Set 98 unordered Boolean b: ArrayOf instance is a Bag 111 sequence Boolean o: Map, MapOf, Record instance is an OrderedSet 67 combine Integer C: Choice instance is an untagged union 47 format String /: Semantic validation keyword 69 scale Integer E: Fixed point scale factor, value * 10^n 116 tagString String t: Table for tag-string serialization 97 abstract Boolean a: Type is not instantiable as a value 102 final Boolean f: Type cannot have subtypes 101 extends String e: Type is a superset of referenced type 114 restricts String r: Type is a subset of referenced type 65 attr Boolean A: Data value may be an XML attribute
ID FieldOption Type ID Description ___ __________ _______ ___ __________________________________________ 91 minOccurs Integer [: min cardinality, default = 1 93 maxOccurs Integer ]: max cardinality, default = 1 48 nillable Boolean 0: Instance may have no value 38 tagId Integer &: field that specifies tagged union type 60 dir Boolean <: path enum treats field as collection 75 key Boolean K: field is the primary key for TypeName 76 link Boolean L: field links to an instance of FieldType

section.name: Primitive Types
section.t: A primitive type has no substructure, and specifies an unrestricted space of atomic values without regard to processing mechanisms or data format. Options applicable to each type limit the value space; if more than one option is present the value space is their intersection.
JADN defines the following primitive types:

ul.li: Boolean: a value from the set of two possible states.
Integer: a value from the discrete whole number line.
Number: a value from the continuous real number line.
String: a sequence of characters.
Binary: a sequence of octets.

section.name: Integer
section.t: An integer is often said to be a number with no fractional part. Integer and number are distinct types, both mathematically and computationally, because a countably infinite set of discrete values differs from a continuum. 1.01 has a fractional part, as does 1.00000000001, as does 1 + 10^-n for every non-infinite positive n. For this reason every decimal character string and every IEEE-754 floating point value that represents an integer, regardless of precision, also represents an infinite number of real values with a fractional part. It is impossible for the number type to uniquely represent an integer without consuming infinite resources.

section.name: Packages
section.t: A JADN package is the mechanism used to organize IM types into groups. It serves as a namespace, meaning types within a package must have unique names, and supports modularization by breaking down large models into manageable subunits, including libraries of sharable types.
JADN packaging is based on XML namespaces:

ul.li: A namespace is identified by a Uniform Resource Identifier (URI), which is a unique name, not necessarily a link to a network resource.

section.name: Shortcuts
section.t: JADN defines a set of core definitions plus several shortcuts that make IM schemas more adaptable or less redundant. Shortcuts are syntactic sugar implemented using options that can be replaced with additional types without affecting functionality. Expanding shortcuts into core definitions simplifies serialization and validation code, and viewing the expanded/simplified schema may aid understanding of the IM.
The shortcuts and the options used to implement them are:

ul.li: : any TypeOption present in FieldOptions
: minOccurs, maxOccurs
: enum, path, dir
: extends, restricts, abstract, final

section.name: Anonymous Types
section.t: This shortcut allows fields within a structured type to be defined without a TypeName. Expanding the definition generates an explicitly path-named type for each anonymous field, moves all TypeOptions included in FieldOptions to the generated type, and replaces FieldType with the TypeName of the generated type. FieldType must be a non-structured CoreType, and any TypeOption included in FieldOptions must apply to FieldType.
Example: a structured type with anonymous fields:
section.sourcecode: Coordinate = Record // A GPS coordinate 1 latitude Number=[-90.0, 90.0] // lat: -90 to 90 degrees 2 longitude Number=(-180.0, 180.0] // long: -179.9999 to 180 degrees
section.t: Expanding the type generates two named types and replaces FieldType with their names. This requires a naming convention for generating path names as discussed in . Path components may be either field IDs ("Coordinate.1") or field names ("Coordinate.latitude"). Writing an IM using explicitly named types (e.g., "Latitude") avoids using this shortcut.
section.sourcecode:
Coordinate = Record // A GPS coordinate 1 latitude Coordinate.latitude // lat: -90 to 90 degrees 2 longitude Coordinate.longitude // long: -179.9999 to 180 degrees
Coordinate.latitude = Number=[-90.0, 90.0] Coordinate.longitude = Number=(-180.0, 180.0]

section.name: Multiplicity
section.t: Fields may be defined to have multiple values of the same type using the minOccurs and maxOccurs FieldOptions. This supports multiplicity notation (1..*), which is universally used in diagram formats. Expanding this shortcut converts each field that can have more than one value to a separate path-named ArrayOf type, but leaves single-valued fields (maxOccurs=1 by default) unchanged. The default minOccurs=1 indicates a required field, while specifying minOccurs=0 indicates that the field is optional.

If an optional field instance with no elements should be represented by an empty list rather than omitted entirely, the IM defines an explicitly-named ArrayOf type in a required field instead of using this shortcut.

section.name: Schema Serialization
section.t: ...non-normative examples...

section.name: Data Serialization
section.t: A JADN IM is based on several equivalence mechanisms that can be included in any data format. This enables a single IM to ... CBOR goals ...

ul.li: Indexing Record elements by position or name
Enumerating data elements by numeric ID or string name / value
Defining string formats for non-string information values

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This memo includes no request to IANA.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: This document should not affect the security of the Internet.

front.title: XML Schema Definition Language (XSD) 1.1 Part 2: Datatypes

section.name: Appendix 1
section.t: This becomes an Appendix

section.name: Acknowledgements

section.name: Contributors
section.t: Thanks to all the contributors.
